file_name: Qi et al. - 2023 - Classification of skin cancer based on hyperspectral microscopic imaging and machine learning.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning methods, specifically Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and Random Forest (RF), to classify skin cancers (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and malignant melanoma) based on hyperspectral microscopic imaging data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results, including data collection of 136 skin tissue cases (BCC, SCC, MM) using a hyperspectral microscopic imaging system, feature extraction, dimensionality reduction, and classification using machine learning models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF). It reports specific accuracy and KAPPA metrics derived from these experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of skin cancer based on hyperspectral microscopic imaging and machine learning
brief_summary: This study classified basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and malignant melanoma using hyperspectral microscopic imaging data from 136 skin tissue cases. Synthetic RGB images were generated from the hyperspectral cube, and features including color moment, GLCM, and LBP were extracted and reduced using partial least squares. Four machine learning models—ELM, SVM, decision tree, and random forest—were evaluated using a hold-out validation method. The SVM model achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 0.791±0.060 and a KAPPA value of 0.685±0.095. The results demonstrate that combining hyperspectral microscopic imaging with machine learning can effectively assist in skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three skin cancer types classified in the study; 34 cases were included in the dataset.
One of the three skin cancer types classified in the study; 63 cases were included in the dataset.
One of the three skin cancer types classified in the study; 39 cases were included in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Malignant melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin tissue samples into three discrete diagnostic categories: basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and malignant melanoma (MM). The study evaluates four machine learning models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF) for this classification task, reporting accuracy and KAPPA metrics. The best performance was achieved by the SVM model with an accuracy of 0.791±0.060.

datasets_used.name: Hyperspectral Microscopic Imaging (HMI) Skin Tissue Dataset
datasets_used.description: A private dataset of skin tissue samples collected using a home-made push-broom HMI system (wavelength range 465.5-905.1 nm, 151 bands). Samples were purchased from Xi'an Alenabio company (sample No: SK801c) and ZhongkeGuanghua (Xi'an) Intelligent Biotechnology company (sample No: K683501). Synthetic RGB images were generated from the HMI data cubes for feature extraction.

use.training: Used for training machine learning models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF) after dividing the dataset via the hold-out method.
use.validation: Used for validation via the hold-out method (specific split counts not reported).
use.testing: Used for testing machine learning models via the hold-out method (specific split counts not reported).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the sample sets into training and testing datasets for model evaluation.
To reduce the dimensionality of the extracted image features (color moment, GLCM, LBP) by selecting principal components with higher contribution rates.
To reduce the dimensionality of the HMI datasets and compare contribution rates with PLS.
To report the uncertainty and variability of classification accuracy and KAPPA values across the hold-out validation splits.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy and KAPPA metrics with mean ± standard deviation derived from the hold-out validation method.

experiment_1_feature_selection_SVM.description: Classification results using SVM model with various single and combined image features (Color moment, HSV, HOG, GLCM, LBP) after PLS dimensionality reduction and hold-out splitting.
experiment_1_feature_selection_SVM.notes: HOG feature was discarded as unreliable due to lowest accuracy and KAPPA. The combination of Color moment, GLCM, and LBP yielded the highest performance.

experiment_2_model_comparison_best_featu.description: Classification results comparing ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, and RF models using the optimal feature set (Color moment, GLCM, LBP) with PLS dimensionality reduction and hold-out splitting.
experiment_2_model_comparison_best_featu.notes: SVM achieved the highest comprehensive performance. A specific result for SVM is also noted as Accuracy 0.802 and KAPPA 0.699 in the text description of Figure 3, though Table 2 reports 0.791±0.060 and 0.685±0.095.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) was the primary model reported as achieving the highest performance. Other models evaluated included Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Decision Tree, and Random Forest (RF).
architecture_used.input_size: Synthetic RGB images generated from hyperspectral data cubes (bands 74, 23, and 1 corresponding to wavelengths 700.1 nm, 545.3 nm, and 465.5 nm respectively). Specific pixel dimensions are not reported.

architecture_details.dimensionality_reduction: Partial Least Squares (PLS) was selected over PCA. First 3 principal components for color moment, HSV, and GLCM; first 10 for HOG and LBP.

training_details.data_splitting: Hold-out method (specific split ratios not reported)

preprocessing_techniques: Synthetic RGB image generation from hyperspectral data cubes by selecting specific bands (band 74 at 700.1 nm for Red, band 23 at 545.3 nm for Green, and band 1 at 465.5 nm for Blue) based on International Commission on Illumination primary color wavelengths.

Dimensionality reduction using Partial Least Squares (PLS) to reduce feature dimensions (first 3 principal components for color moment, HSV, and GLCM; first 10 for HOG and LBP) after comparing with Principal Component Analysis (PCA).
Dataset partitioning using the hold-out method to divide sample sets into training and testing datasets.
conclusions: The combination of hyperspectral microscopic imaging (HMI) and machine learning can effectively assist in skin cancer diagnosis with high efficiency and accuracy.
Partial Least Squares (PLS) dimensionality reduction outperformed Principal Component Analysis (PCA) by achieving higher contribution rates with fewer principal components.

The combination of color moment, gray level co-occurrence matrix (GLCM), and local binary pattern (LBP) features yielded the highest classification performance compared to other single or combined features.

The Support Vector Machine (SVM) model achieved the best comprehensive performance among the tested models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF), with an accuracy of 0.791±0.060 and a KAPPA value of 0.685±0.095.
The HOG feature was identified as unreliable due to its lowest classification accuracy and KAPPA value and was subsequently discarded.
The study acknowledges that more tissue samples are needed in future work to test and optimize model performance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, private dataset of only 136 tissue samples purchased from commercial suppliers, raising concerns about generalizability and external validity.
The dataset exhibits class imbalance, with 63 cases of Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) compared to 34 Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and 39 Malignant Melanoma (MM) cases.
The paper acknowledges that more tissue samples are needed in future work to test and optimize model performance, indicating current evidence is preliminary.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using a hold-out method on the internal dataset.
The specific ratio of the hold-out split (training vs. testing) is not reported, which limits reproducibility and assessment of data leakage risks.

The HOG feature was discarded as unreliable due to the lowest classification accuracy (0.454) and KAPPA value (0.164), suggesting potential instability in texture feature extraction for this specific imaging setup.
There is a discrepancy in reported SVM performance: Table 2 reports accuracy 0.791±0.060, while the text describing Figure 3 reports accuracy 0.802 and KAPPA 0.699.
The study uses synthetic RGB images generated from specific hyperspectral bands rather than raw hyperspectral data for feature extraction, which may lose spectral information critical for diagnosis.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals beyond mean ± standard deviation, limiting statistical rigor.
The hardware setup (home-made push-broom HMI system) is proprietary and not standardized, potentially hindering direct comparison with other studies using commercial systems.
